1.2: pasan charan hegesasthe adelphoi mou hotan peirasmois peripesete poikilois (pāsan charān hēgēsasthe, adelphoi mou, hotan peirasmois peripesēte poikilois): 'Count it all joy when you fall into various trials.' Hegeomai (count/consider) is a deliberate act of valuation — joy is not a natural reaction to trials but a chosen re-evaluation. Peirasmoi (trials/temptations) covers both external testing by circumstances (v. 2-4) and internal temptation by desire (v. 13-14) — James distinguishes them but uses the same word, showing that external pressure and internal desire interact in the same human experience of testing.
1.22: ginesthe de poietai logou kai me monon akroatai paralogizomenoi heautous (ginesthe de poiētai logou kai mē monon akroatai paralogizomenoi heautous): 'Be doers of the word and not hearers only, deceiving yourselves.' Poietes logou (doer of the word): the verbal-moral contrast between hearing and doing is fundamental to both the wisdom tradition (Prov 2:1-6) and the dominical teaching (Matt 7:24-27: the wise and foolish builders). Paralogizomenoi (deceiving, reasoning falsely alongside) implies a syllogistic self-deception: 'I heard the word, therefore I have received it and its benefits' — the error of intellectual reception without moral transformation.
1.1:
doulos — James identifies himself as a doulos (servant/slave) of God and of the Lord Jesus Christ. The term is more than a humility marker: in the OT, ebed YHWH (servant of the LORD) is a title of honor given to Moses (Deut 34:5), Joshua (Josh 24:29), and the Servant of Isaiah (Isa 42:1; 53:11). James, the brother of the Lord, adopts the Servant vocabulary rather than claiming familial status — a significant rhetorical and theological choice.

diaspora — the diaspora (scattered abroad) is the technical term for Jews living outside the land of Israel. James writes to tais dōdeka phylais tais en tē diasporā (the twelve tribes in the dispersion). Whether this is a literal address to Jewish-Christian congregations or a metaphorical address to the whole messianic community as God's scattered people (cf. 1 Pet 1:1) is debated; the twelve-tribes language makes a claim about the church as the reconstituted Israel.
1.3: dokimion — to dokimion hymōn tēs pisteōs (the testing of your faith). The noun dokimion is related to dokimos (approved/tested/genuine) and is used for the process of assaying metal to prove its genuineness. In 1 Pet 1:7, the same image appears: faith tested like gold in fire. The MKT translates it 'testing' rather than 'trial' to capture the assay-process meaning: the outcome is not merely hardship endured but faith proved genuine. The byproduct is hypomonē (endurance), not just survival.
1.4: teleios / holoklēros / leipomenoi — the verse stacks three Greek terms for completeness: teleios (mature/perfect/complete), holoklēros (whole/entire/intact — a term used of sacrificial animals with no missing parts), and the negation leipomenoi (lacking, from leipō). James wants hypomenē to complete its 'full work' (ergon teleion) so that the person may be teleios kai holoklēros — mature and intact, lacking nothing. The use of teleios at 1:4 picks up the same word from 1:17 (perfect gift) and 1:25 (perfect law); the word is thematic throughout James.
1.5: haplōs — God gives haplōs (generously/simply/without reservation). This rare adverb (haplos) is from haplos (single, simple, undivided) — the same root as haplous in Matt 6:22 (the single/sound eye). God's giving is not divided, not begrudging, not complicated by conditions that vary from person to person. The contrast with the dipsychos (double-minded, two-souled) of v8 is deliberate: God is the single-minded giver; the doubter is the double-minded asker. Wisdom is available from God without the kind of internal division that characterizes the wavering person.
1.6: klydōni thalassēs anemizomenō kai rhipizomenō — the doubter is like a wave of the sea (klydōni — surge, billow) being anemizomenō (wind-driven) and rhipizomenō (tossed). Both participles are present tense, depicting continuous agitation. The sea-wave image for instability and lack of grounding is an OT wisdom image (cf. Isa 57:20: 'the wicked are like the tossing sea, which cannot rest'). James uses physical instability as the metaphor for the psychological/spiritual instability of the person who simultaneously asks and doubts whether the asking will work.
1.7: ekeinēs — 'that person' (ekeinēs) is demonstratively distancing — pointing to the doubter as a case to be considered from the outside. The double negative mē oiesthō hoti lēmpsetai ti (let them not suppose that they will receive anything) uses oiesthō (suppose/think) for a kind of wishful or unfounded expectation. The person who both asks and doubts has not made the decisive commitment to trust that prayer requires; they are hedging, and hedging prayer produces nothing.
1.8: dipsychos — one of the most discussed rare Greek words in the NT. Dipsychos (double-souled/double-minded) appears in the NT only in James (1:8; 4:8). It may be James's own coinage or derived from Jewish wisdom tradition (cf. Testament of Asher 3:2 on the two-faced person). The compound di (two) + psychē (soul/self) depicts internal division — the person whose will is split between trust and doubt, between God and the world. The word becomes a technical term for wavering in early Christian literature (especially the Shepherd of Hermas), suggesting James introduced it into Christian vocabulary.
1.9: tapeinosis — the rich person should boast in their tapeinōsis (humiliation/low position). This is an inversion: wealth normally produces hypsos (height/honor); James says the rich should glory in their tapeinōsis, the exact word for the downward social trajectory that wealth loss brings. The reversal vocabulary is consistent with the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3) and the Magnificat (Luke 1:48: epeblepsen epi tēn tapeinōsin tēs doulēs autou) — those who are humbled are exalted, and vice versa.
1.10: anthos chortou — anthos (blossom/flower) + chortos (grass/hay) — the same image used in Isa 40:6-8 ('all flesh is grass, and all its glory is like the flower of the field; the grass withers, the flower fades'). James is deliberately echoing Isaiah's great text on the transience of human power. The rich person will 'pass away' (maranthesetai, wither/fade) like the grass-flower: not merely die but be visibly reduced, the verb catching the visual process of wilting. The hot wind that withers in v11 is the kapson (scorching heat/sirocco wind) from the east — a detail of Levantine climate.
1.11: kapson — the scorching east wind (kapson; also in Matt 20:12; Luke 12:55) is the sirocco wind that blows from the Arabian desert. It does not merely dry out; it shrivels living vegetation in hours. The phrase exēranen ton chorton kai to anthos autou exepesen (withered the grass and its flower fell off) describes a visible sudden end — the same speed that wealth can disappear. Euprепeia (beauty/good appearance) in 'its beauty perishes' is a hapax in the NT; it is the term for beautiful/noble appearance — the outward excellence that people associate with wealth and that the sirocco eliminates instantly.
1.12:
makarios — the beatitude form (blessed is the man who endures trial, makarios anēr hypomenei peirasmon) echoes Ps 1:1 and the Beatitudes. Makarios in Greek has a range from 'happy' to 'blessed by God'; in wisdom literature it describes the state of the person who is rightly oriented toward God and his purposes.

stephanos tēs zōēs — the crown of life (stephanos = victor's wreath, not a royal crown which would be diadema). In the Greco-Roman world, stephanos was the athletic prize and the civic honor-crown. James uses the civic/athletic image but spiritualizes the recipient: the one who has loved God (agapōsin auton) — a participle describing ongoing disposition, not merely a past decision.
1.13: apeirastos kakōn — God 'cannot be tempted by evil' (apeirastos kakōn). The adjective apeirastos is a hapax in the NT and rare in Greek literature. It is likely passive in meaning ('not subject to being tempted/tested by evil') rather than active ('unable to test with evil'). The point is not that God lacks power to test but that evil has no purchase on God — he has no internal inclination toward it that could be exploited. This is the ground for the argument of v14: since God himself is not inclined toward evil, any evil inclination must have its origin in the human person.
1.14: epithymias / deleazomenos — each person is tempted when 'drawn away' (exelkomenos — dragged out from a place of safety) and 'enticed' (deleazomenos — the latter word is a fishing term meaning 'lured with bait'; cf. 2 Pet 2:14, 18 where it appears for those who entice new converts). The personification of epithymia (desire/lust/craving) as an active agent that draws and baits the person is vivid: desire is not portrayed as passive but as a hunter. The origin is internal (hypo tēs idias epithymias, by one's own desire) — emphatically one's own.
1.15: syllabousa... apotiktei... apotelesthēisa — a sequence of three aorist participles building a biological metaphor of conception, birth, and maturity: desire conceives (syllabousa, lit. 'having taken together' — the technical term for conception), gives birth to sin (apotiktei), and sin, when fully grown (apotelesthēisa, having reached its full end/maturity), gives birth to death. The chain is methodical: desire → sin → death, echoing the death-pattern established in Gen 2:17. The word apotelesthēisa (completed/come to full term) is rare; it catches the idea of sin reaching its terminal maturity.
1.16: planasthe — do not be deceived (mē planāsthe, present passive imperative) frames what follows as a warning against a common error of thinking. The verb planaō (wander/be led astray) is the root of planē (error/deception). The command is to stop being led astray — implying the tendency is already present. The contrast that follows (v17) is the truth that corrects the error: the character of God as giver of good rather than source of temptation.
1.17: aposkiasma tropēs — one of the most beautiful phrases in James: God is the Father of lights with whom there is parallagē ē tropēs aposkiasma — no variation or shadow due to turning. Parallagē (variation/alteration) is an astronomical term for the apparent change in a celestial body's position; tropē (turning) is used for the solstice (the sun's 'turning' point); aposkiasma (shadow from) is the shadow produced by the eclipse or the movement of a heavenly body. James uses astronomical precision to describe divine constancy: the Father of the heavenly lights (sun, moon, stars) is himself not subject to the variations those lights undergo. His character and giving do not shift like a shadow.
1.18: aparchē — James describes believers as aparchēn tina tōn autou ktismatōn (a kind of firstfruits of his creatures). The term aparchē (firstfruits) is the technical Levitical term for the first and representative portion of a harvest offered to God, which consecrates the whole (Lev 23:10; Num 15:20-21; Rom 11:16). By calling believers 'firstfruits,' James places the church within the redemptive-harvest framework: they are the first installment of God's new-creation harvest, with the implication that more will follow. The 'word of truth' by which he gave them birth echoes the creation-by-word motif (Gen 1; Ps 33:6) applied to new creation.
1.19: tachy... bradys... bradys — the famous triad: quick to hear (tachy eis to akouein), slow to speak (bradys eis to lalein), slow to anger (bradys eis orgēn). The Greek word order highlights the contrast: two 'quicks' and one 'slow' would make the typical person's natural tendency the opposite of what James commands. The asymmetry (one fast, two slow) teaches the priority of listening. The command is set up by v18's 'word of truth': having been 'given birth' by that word, the appropriate posture toward all words is careful reception before rapid production.
1.20: orgē andros — human anger (orgē andros, literally 'man's wrath' — anēr is the male-specific noun rather than the generic anthrōpos) does not accomplish (ergazetai, work/produce) the dikaiosynēn theou (righteousness of God). The phrase 'righteousness of God' could mean the righteousness God requires, or the righteous state God produces, or God's own righteousness. In this context it likely means 'the righteous conduct that God desires' — human anger is counterproductive to the community life James envisions. The andros specificity may be a realistic observation about where anger was most commonly exercised.
1.21: emphyton logon — accept the implanted word (ton emphyton logon dexasthe). The adjective emphytos (implanted/innate) appears only here in the NT. It is used in Greek philosophy for inborn knowledge; here James applies it to the word of truth (v18) that has been planted/implanted in the believer through the new birth. The contrast is with filth (rJyparia) and surplus of evil (perisseia kakias) that is to be removed. The image suggests the word is not external information but seed already present within, waiting to be cultivated by the humble (prautēti, meekness/gentleness) reception of it.
1.23: en esoptrō — the mirror (esoptron) metaphor is used to describe the hearer-only who looks at their natural face (prosōpon tēs genesēos autou, literally 'the face of his birth/origin') in a mirror. The mirror in the ancient world was polished bronze — not a glass mirror — and its image was imperfect and fleeting. The image of seeing one's face in a mirror was proverbial for self-examination; James's point is that the examination that does not lead to change is as transient as a glance that produces no retention.
1.24: katenoēsen kai apelelythen — he looked (katanoeō, attentively observed/considered) and has gone away (aperchomai, departed). The sequence of aorists (both action and departure) emphasizes the brevity: the looking and the going away happen rapidly without any lasting effect. The perfect epelēlathen (has forgotten) indicates a current state resulting from past action: the forgetting has already taken hold and persists. The point is not that careful observation is worthless but that observation without formation produces self-deception.
1.25:
nomos teleios tēs eleutherias — the 'perfect law of liberty/freedom' (nomos teleios tēs eleutherias). All three words are loaded: nomos (law) used here is not Torah in the Pauline polemical sense but instruction/principle; teleios (perfect/mature/complete) picks up the theme from 1:4; eleutheria (freedom) is a term with strong Stoic associations (the truly free person is the one who acts consistently with reason) but James uses it for freedom from the disordered desires described in vv14-15. The 'perfect law of liberty' is the implanted word (v21) that, when obeyed, produces genuine freedom rather than the servitude of desire.

parakyptein — looks intently (parakyptein) — the same verb used in 1 Pet 1:12 for angels stooping to look into the gospel mysteries. It describes a careful, attentive bending over to examine something closely — the exact opposite of the mirror-glance of v23-24.
1.26: thrēskos / thrēskeia — religion (thrēskeia) and religious (thrēskos) are rare words in the NT (only here in James and in Acts 26:5; Col 2:18). The word-group denotes the outward practice of religion — rites, ceremonies, forms of devotion. James uses it here and in v27 to make a sharp distinction: religion that does not control the tongue is worthless (mataios, empty/futile/vain), regardless of outward piety. The irony is that thrēskeia normally referred to careful cultic observance; James inverts it: the truly careful religious person controls speech and serves the vulnerable.
1.27: katharos kai amiantos — the pure and undefiled religion: katharos (clean/pure — the standard cultic purity term) and amiantos (undefiled/unstained — used in Heb 7:26 for Jesus as the perfect high priest). James uses the Levitical purity vocabulary to describe not ritual cleanliness but the social righteousness of caring for orphans and widows. The 'world' from which one keeps oneself 'unstained' (aspilon) is not physical creation but the world-system of disordered values. Three purity terms in one verse redirect the concept of ritual cleanness toward relational and ethical cleanness.

2.14: ti ophelos adelphoi mou ean pistin lege tis echein erga de me eche (ti ophelos, adelphoi mou, ean pistin legē tis echein, erga de mē echē): 'What good is it, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works?' The famous James-Paul tension: Luther notoriously called James 'an epistle of straw' for seeming to contradict Paul's faith-righteousness. The resolution lies in different uses of pistis: Paul's 'faith' is the whole person's trust in and surrender to God; James's target is a merely verbal profession (legē tis echein, 'says he has faith') without ethical corollary. James and Paul agree that genuine faith produces works (cf. Gal 5:6: 'faith working through love'); their different opponents require different emphases.
2.1: prosōpolēmpsiais — favoritism (prosōpolēmpsiais, plural of prosōpolēmpsia) is a compound: prosōpon (face) + lambanō (to receive/take). The LXX translates the Hebrew nasa panim ('to lift the face') with this compound — to accept/respect someone based on their outward appearance or social position. The OT prohibits this in legal and social contexts (Deut 1:17; 10:17; Lev 19:15). James applies the prohibition to the assembly context. The plural (prosōpolēmpsiais) is unusual — perhaps emphasizing the many specific instances that constitute the attitude.
2.2: synagōgē — James uses synagōgē (synagogue/assembly) for the Christian meeting, not ekklēsia (the more common NT word for church). This is one of the only NT uses of synagōgē for a Christian gathering (cf. also Heb 10:25's episynagōgē). It may indicate the Jewish-Christian character of James's audience — they still used the Jewish vocabulary for their assembly. chrysodaktylios (gold-ringed) is a compound hapax: wearing a gold ring on the finger was a sign of equestrian rank in Rome; it signals wealth at a glance.
2.3: epiblepō — you look with special regard (epiblepsēte, aorist subjunctive of epiblepō) on the man in fine clothes. The verb means 'to look upon with special attention' — it can be positive (God looking favorably on the humble, Luke 1:48) or here negative (looking with preference on the wealthy). The spatial metaphor continues: 'here is a good seat for you' vs 'stand there' or 'sit at my feet' (hypokato tou hypopodio mou, under my footstool) — the foot-position is an extreme social degradation, normally used for slaves or conquered enemies.
2.4: diekrithēte — you have discriminated/made distinctions (diekrithēte, passive of diakrinō). The same root as diakrisis (discernment); here it is making distinctions that should not be made — social sorting within the covenant community. 'Judges with evil thoughts' (kritai dialogismōn ponērōn) — dialogismos usually means reasoning/thought but here has the sense of judicial reasoning that is ponērōn (evil/corrupt). They have set themselves up as judges and their judging principle is corrupt because it mirrors the world's social hierarchy.
2.5: tous ptōchous tō kosmō, plousious en pistei — the Greek antithesis is sharp: poor in the world (ptōchous tō kosmō, dative of sphere — poor with respect to the world's measure), rich in faith (plousious en pistei). The poor whom the world despises are the rich ones in God's economy, heirs of the kingdom (klēronomous tēs basileias). Klēronomos (heir) is a legal term for one who inherits by right; James's claim is that the poor who love God have a legal claim on the kingdom.
2.6: ētimāsate — you have dishonored (ētimāsate, aorist of atimazō). The root is timē (honor); atimazō is to strip of honor, to treat as without worth. The indictment is that the Jamesian community has done exactly what the world does: respected wealth and stripped honor from poverty, precisely reversing the divine evaluation of v5. The rhetorical questions that follow (v6b, v7) build the irony: the wealthy whom you honor are the very ones dragging you into courts and blaspheming the name.
2.7: to kalon onoma to epiklēthen eph' hymas — 'the noble name called over you' (to kalon onoma to epiklēthen eph' hymas). The phrase 'the name called over' comes from the LXX formula epikaleō to onoma kyriou — the name of YHWH invoked over Israel as his possession (Deut 28:10; Amos 9:12; Acts 15:17). To have the name called over you is to be claimed as belonging to the one whose name it is. Blaspheming this name is not merely insulting Christians but attacking the Lord to whom they belong. Kalon (noble/beautiful/good) emphasizes the worth of the name being defamed.
2.8: nomos basilikos — the royal law (nomos basilikos, literally 'kingly law'). The title is unique in the NT. It likely means the supreme law that governs the whole of covenant life — the royal commandment that functions as the organizing principle of all other law. James identifies it as the Lev 19:18 command ('Love your neighbor as yourself'), the same text Jesus identified as the 'great commandment' (Matt 22:39; Mark 12:31). The kata tēn graphēn (according to the Scripture) grounds the command in its OT locus rather than in Jesus's citation alone.
2.9: prosōpolēmpteite... elenchomenoi hypo tou nomou hōs parabatai — if you show partiality, you commit sin (hamartian ergazesthe, you work/produce sin — the same verb as 1:20 'produce righteousness') and are convicted (elenchomenoi — to be shown up, exposed, convicted by evidence) by the law as transgressors (parabatai, those who step over the boundary/violate). The legal register is prominent: the law functions as prosecutor that demonstrates the violation. The person who shows favoritism has violated the royal law of v8, making them a lawbreaker rather than a keeper.
2.10: holōn nomon tērein... heni de ptaisē — whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles (ptaisē, from ptaiō — to trip/stumble, used for a misstep rather than a fall; the same word in v2 and 3:2) at one point has become liable (gegonan enachos) for all. The legal logic is that Torah is a unified system, not a collection of independent rules — its integrity depends on the unity of the lawgiver (v11: the one who said both prohibitions is the same God). Violating any commandment is a relational offense against the unified will of the one God, not merely a statistical infraction.
2.11: ho eipon... eipen — the same one who said ('you shall not commit adultery') also said ('you shall not murder'). The argument grounds the unity of the law in the unity of the lawgiver: God is the single will behind both commandments, so violating one is an offense against the whole person of God, not merely that single rule. The order — adultery mentioned before murder — is unusual (normally murder first, cf. Exod 20:13-14); it may be the order of Deut 5:17-18 or simply James's order, possibly citing the commandment seen as more 'minor' (adultery) before the obviously serious one (murder) to underscore that both carry equal weight.
2.12: dia nomou eleutherias krinesthai — to be judged through the law of freedom (dia nomou eleutherias). The same phrase as 1:25 (nomos teleios tēs eleutherias). James frames judgment in terms of the 'perfect law of liberty' — not the law as enslaving code but the word of truth (1:18, 21) that liberates from disordered desire. Speaking and acting should be done in the awareness that the criterion of final judgment is this freedom-producing law: have you lived and spoken in ways that reflect the liberation from partiality and selfish desire that the word produces?
2.13: katakauchatai eleos kriseos — mercy triumphs over judgment (katakauchatai eleos kriseos, literally 'mercy boasts against/over judgment'). The verb katakauchaō means 'to boast against' or 'to triumph over' — it appears in Rom 11:18 (Gentiles should not boast against the branches) and is a strong, almost combative term. Mercy is not merely the mitigating factor that reduces the penalty; it actively overwhelms judgment. The same word appears in James 3:14: James uses it for self-glorification that overrides truth. Here mercy is the active force that overrides the judgment-consequence for those who have shown it.
2.15: gymnoi hyparchōsin kai leipomenoi tēs ephēmerou trophēs — naked (gymnoi — without adequate clothing, not literally unclothed) and lacking (leipomenoi, the same verb as 1:4 — deficient, short of what is needed) daily food (ephēmerou trophēs — provision for the day, daily bread). The example is deliberately domestic: not the dramatic poor but the everyday need for clothing and food. The community James describes contains both the wealthy (2:1-7) and those with these basic needs unmet — the contrast underscores the practical urgency of works.
2.16: hypagete en eirēnē — go in peace (hypagete en eirēnē) — the standard Jewish farewell (shalom, peace be with you), now rendered hollow by the refusal to provide physical necessities. The phrase exposes the gap between religious speech and enacted care: the blessing is real vocabulary but empty of the substance that would make it a genuine blessing. Ta epitēdeia tou sōmatos (the necessities of the body) — the word epitēdeia is a plural noun for necessary provisions; the body's needs are concrete and cannot be met by correct speech.
2.17: nekra kath' heautēn — the claim that faith without works is dead (nekra) 'in itself' (kath' heautēn) — by itself, alone, considered in isolation. The contrast is not between a dead faith and no faith but between faith that stands alone (without works) and faith that is accompanied by works. The word nekra is the same root as the death that sin produces in 1:15; James is drawing on the same life/death vocabulary. A dead faith is not merely ineffective; it lacks the animating principle (works = evidence of genuine trust) that makes it alive.
2.18: deiksō ek tōn ergōn mou tēn pistin — show me your faith apart from works and I will show you my faith by my works (deiksō ek tōn ergōn mou tēn pistin mou). The Greek makes the point epistemologically: faith is not directly observable; works are. The only way to demonstrate faith is through its visible expression in action. Chōris tōn ergōn (apart from/without works) — chōris means separated from, spatially apart. A faith spatially removed from any evidence in action is not demonstrable, hence not faith.
2.19: heis estin ho theos — 'there is one God' (heis estin ho theos) — the Shema (Deut 6:4: shema yisrael YHWH elohenu YHWH echad). The monotheistic confession is the foundational Jewish creed; James uses it to cut off a certain kind of boast: assenting to the Shema is not itself the distinguishing mark of genuine faith, since the demons (daimonia) do the same. The word phrisso (shudder/tremble) is vivid — the hair standing up on end in terror. The demons' response to the Shema is more overtly visceral than that of many nominal believers: they at least exhibit physical acknowledgment of the implication of monotheism.
2.20: argē — faith without works is argē (idle/barren/useless). The word means literally 'not working' (a- + ergon, work), which makes the wordplay explicit: faith without 'works' (ergōn) is itself 'not-working' (argē). Some manuscripts read nekra (dead) instead of argē at this point, assimilating to v17 and v26; the word argē is the harder reading and likely original, adding a new angle: barrenness/uselessness rather than death.
2.21: dikaiōthē — Abraham was justified (edikaiōthē, aorist passive of dikaioō) when he offered Isaac. The aorist refers to the Genesis 22 event. This has long been compared to Paul's use of the same term for Abraham in Rom 4:3 (citing Gen 15:6). The key distinction is that James uses dikaioō for a visible vindication that followed from works, while Paul uses it for a forensic declaration that preceded circumcision and preceded any work. They are not using the word in an identical sense: Paul's justification is the initial declaration; James's is the public demonstration/vindication of an already-established righteousness. Neither denies the other's point.
2.22: synērgei — his faith was working together with (synērgei, imperfect active of synergei — to work together, cooperate) his works. The imperfect tense depicts continuous cooperative action during the testing. The works did not produce the faith or replace it; they 'co-worked' with it. Eteleiōthē (was made complete/perfected, aorist passive of teleioō) — faith reached its completion through the works. This picks up the teleios theme from 1:4: the maturity/completeness that endurance produces is precisely what faith becomes when it works out its logic in action.
2.23: eplērōthē hē graphē — the scripture was fulfilled (eplērōthē, aorist passive of plēroō) that says Abraham believed God and it was reckoned to him as righteousness. The citation is Gen 15:6, the same text Paul cites in Gal 3 and Rom 4. James's point is that the Gen 22 event (Abraham's offering of Isaac) was the fulfillment of the Gen 15:6 declaration — the initial reckoning of faith as righteousness reached its intended fullness (the meaning of plēroō) when faith was enacted under test. The philos theou (friend of God) title for Abraham is from Isa 41:8 and was a standard Jewish honorific.
2.24: ex ergōn dikaioutai... ouk ek pisteōs monon — justified by works and not by faith alone (ouk ek pisteōs monon). The adverb monon (alone) is the key: James does not say faith is irrelevant, but that faith alone — isolated from works — is not the ground of justification in the sense of visible vindication. The ex ergōn (from works) describes the evidentiary basis by which one is 'declared righteous' before the community or in the final judgment; Paul's ek pisteōs (from faith) describes the forensic basis before God at the initial moment of justification.
2.25: Hraab hē pornē — Rahab the prostitute (hē pornē) — the Greek word pornē is the standard term for a commercial sex worker. James does not soften the descriptor; it is part of the argument: the unlikeliest of people is declared righteous by her works. Hypodexaménē (having received/welcomed — a hospitality term) and ekballousa (having sent away by a different route) are the two acts. The faith behind them is implied but not stated; James assumes the reader knows the Rahab narrative (Josh 2; 6:17-25) and its repeated emphasis on her trust in YHWH (Josh 2:9-11).
2.26: chōris pneumatos nekron... chōris tōn ergōn nekra — as the body without the spirit is dead, so faith without works is dead. The analogy uses pneuma for the animating principle of the body — the breath/spirit of life (Gen 2:7: God breathed into Adam's nostrils the breath of life). Works are to faith as breath is to body: the evidence of life, not the source of it but the sign that the animating principle is present. Without breath, the body is a corpse; without works, the faith-claim is a corpse.

3.1: me polloi didaskaloi ginesthe adelphoi mou eidotes hoti meizon krima lepsometha (mē polloi didaskaloi ginesthe, adelphoi mou, eidotes hoti meizon krima lēpsometha): 'Not many of you should become teachers, my brothers, knowing that we who teach will be judged with greater strictness.' The meizon krima (greater judgment) for teachers reflects the Jewish wisdom tradition's high estimation of the Torah-teacher's responsibility (m. Avot 2:7: 'the more Torah, the more life; the more study, the more wisdom; the more counsel, the more understanding; the more righteousness, the more peace; ... the more Torah, the more life') and its warning about the consequences of teaching falsehood. James is aware of his own exposure to this judgment.
3.2: teleios anēr — the one who does not stumble in speech is a teleios anēr (perfect/complete/mature man). The connection to the teleios of 1:4 is deliberate: the mature person of ch.1 was to be produced by endurance; here, control of the tongue is the marker of that maturity. The able-to-bridle-the-whole-body metaphor connects speech control to bodily self-governance: mastering the most unruly member (tongue) demonstrates the capacity to master the rest. Kai holon to sōma chalinagōgēsai — the ability to 'bridle the whole body' (chalinagōgēsai, hapax in the NT; from chalinos, bridle + agō, to lead).
3.3: chalinous eis ta stomata tōn hippōn — bits/bridles into the mouths of horses. The argument moves from the small bit that controls the large horse, to the small rudder that steers the large ship (v4), to the small tongue that sets the course of the whole person's life. The rhetorical technique is synkrisis (comparison of unlike things by their ratio): small controlling large is the pattern that makes the tongue's influence comprehensible. The term metago (we direct their whole bodies) uses a compound that emphasizes transfer: the bit transfers the rider's will to the horse's entire movement.
3.4: euthynōn hormē — the ship is steered by a very small rudder wherever the impulse of the one steering wills (hopou hē hormē tou euthynontos bouletai). Hormē (impulse/inclination) is the navigator's directing intention translated into the rudder's movement. The nautical term euthynōn (one who steers straight) may echo the same nautical vocabulary in Greek wisdom literature about self-governance (Stoic metaphors of the steering reason). James's argument is that the rudder of speech similarly encodes the soul's deepest inclinations and translates them into life's direction.
3.5: glōssa mikron melos estin kai megala auchei — the tongue is a small member and yet boasts great things (megala auchei). The verb auchei (boasts/brags) — from aucheo — depicts speech not merely as communication but as self-assertion. The forest-fire image (hylikon pyr anaptei, sets a great forest on fire) is vivid: James has just described the tongue's disproportionate influence (small bit, small rudder, small tongue) and now captures the destructive version of that disproportionality. The small match ignites a wildfire that cannot be recalled.
3.6: kosmos tēs adikias / ton trochon tēs geneseos — the tongue is a 'world of unrighteousness' (kosmos tēs adikias) among our members and 'sets on fire the wheel of existence' (ton trochon tēs geneseos phlogizousa). The second phrase is the most debated in James: trochon tēs geneseos (wheel of genesis/becoming/birth) may echo the Orphic doctrine of the wheel of reincarnation, or more plausibly the cycle of human life from birth to death. The tongue defiles (spilousa, stains) the whole body, sets the whole course of life ablaze, and is itself set on fire by Gehenna (geenna, the only NT use of geenna outside the Gospels in this sense).
3.7: physis tōn thēriōn te kai peteinōn — every kind of animal — beasts, birds, reptiles, sea creatures — has been tamed (damazetai, tamed/subdued) and is being tamed by human physis (nature). The fourfold list echoes the four categories of Gen 1:26-28 over which God gave humanity dominion (creatures of the land, air, ground-creeping, sea). James's point is ironic: humanity exercises the Gen 1 dominion mandate over the entire animal creation but cannot tame its own tongue.
3.8: akatastaton kakon gemei iou thanatēphorou — an unruly/restless evil (akatastaton kakon — akatastatos appears in 1:8 for the double-minded person; the tongue has the same instability), full (gemei) of deadly poison (iou thanatēphorou — ios means both poison/venom and rust; here the death-bearing poison of a serpent or arrow). The tongue's self-description as 'full of deadly poison' echoes the Psalm tradition: Ps 140:3 ('they make their tongues as sharp as a serpent's; the poison of vipers is on their lips') and Ps 58:4 ('their venom is like the venom of a snake').
3.9: kath' homoiōsin theou — made in the likeness (homoiōsis) of God. The term picks up Gen 1:26-27 (both eikōn and homoiōsis are used in the LXX of Gen 1:26). To curse a person made in God's likeness is to curse the image of God — it is an indirect blasphemy against God himself. This makes the double-use of the tongue (blessing God, cursing image-bearers) internally incoherent: to use the same mouth for both is to address the same divine reality with opposite attitudes, which is exactly the dipsychos (double-mindedness) James condemns throughout the letter.
3.10: ou chrē... ginesthai — this should not be (ou chrē, it is not fitting/proper/necessary — a mild but firm expression of inappropriateness). The grammar is impersonal (chrē, 'it is fitting') rather than direct imperative, making the observation almost logical: these things ought not to coexist in nature. The blending of blessing and cursing from one source is as unnatural as a spring producing both fresh and salt water (v11) or a tree producing incompatible fruits (v12). The moral impossibility is argued by analogy to natural impossibility.
3.11: glykos kai pikron — sweet water and bitter (glykys kai pikros) from the same spring. The paradox of natural springs is used as an analogy for the tongue's incompatible productions. In the ancient world, the freshwater/saltwater boundary was a real concern for wells near the sea coast; a spring that produced both would be useless. Brein (to gush/bubble forth) is a vivid verb for the natural overflow of a spring — the tongue should naturally overflow with consistent water.
3.12: elasthē hypo sklērou anemou — although driven by fierce winds (sklērou anemou, hard/harsh wind). The same contrast of size and power as v3-4: the ship is large, the winds are fierce, the rudder is small. James's consistency in using nautical and natural imagery makes the tongue-section a unified argument from proportion and nature to the disproportionate power and inconsistency of speech. The fig tree and grapevine image (oute ampelon syka poiēsai) draws on the OT's frequent use of vineyards and fig trees as images for Israel's prosperity and faithfulness.
3.13: epistēmōn en hymin — wise and understanding (sophos kai epistēmōn). The pair echoes the OT wisdom vocabulary: Deut 1:13 calls for 'wise, discerning, and experienced men' using the same two Greek words in the LXX (sophos kai epistēmōn). Anastrophē (way of life/conduct) is the word for the total pattern of one's life and social interaction — wisdom is visible in the consistency of the lived pattern, not merely in pronouncements. Prautēti sophias (meekness/gentleness of wisdom) — prautēs appears in 1:21 for the receptivity needed to receive the implanted word; here it is the disposition of genuine wisdom.
3.14: zēlon pikron kai eritheian — bitter jealousy (zēlon pikron) and selfish ambition (eritheia). Eritheia is a word with interesting etymology: originally it meant 'hired labor' and then came to mean the self-seeking behavior of someone working only for personal gain. In Paul it appears among the works of the flesh (Gal 5:20) and the divided motives (Phil 1:17). James pairs it with bitter zeal as the characteristic of the earthly wisdom described in v15. Pseudesththe kata tēs alētheias (lie against the truth) — not merely being untruthful but actively directing falsehood against the truth, treating the truth as an opponent.
3.15: epigios, psychikē, daimonōdēs — three descending terms for earthly wisdom's origin: epigeios (earthly — from the earth, not from above), psychikē (soulish/unspiritual — belonging to the psychē, the natural self, without the Spirit; Paul uses this word in 1 Cor 2:14 for the 'natural' person who cannot receive the Spirit's things), and daimonōdēs (demonic/of demon-character). The three-level descent is from natural to sub-natural to actively malevolent. The wisdom of jealousy and selfish ambition is not merely human but demonic in character — it participates in the disorder introduced into creation by hostile spiritual forces.
3.16: akatastasia kai pan phaulon pragma — disorder (akatastasia — the same root as akatastatos, the unstable double-minded person of 1:8 and the restless evil tongue of 3:8) and every vile/worthless practice (pan phaulon pragma). The three instability-words (akatastatos/akatastasia) form a thread through the letter: individual instability (1:8), verbal/social disorder (3:8), and communal chaos (3:16) are all expressions of the same divided soul that has not submitted to God's wisdom.
3.17: hagnē, eirēnikē, epieikēs, eupeithēs, eleos, agathē, adiakritos, anypokritos — the eight qualities of wisdom from above form a compact ethical vocabulary. Hagnē (pure/chaste — ritual and moral cleanness); eirēnikē (peaceable — peace-producing); epieikēs (gentle/considerate/equitable — the quality of yielding one's strict legal rights for the sake of relationship); eupeithēs (open to reason/compliant/ready to yield — not the stubbornness of selfish ambition); eleos kai karpous agathous (mercy and good fruits — practical expressions of wisdom in community); adiakritos (impartial/without making invidious distinctions — the opposite of 2:1-7); anypokritos (sincere/without hypocrisy — literally 'not acting'). The sequence from pure to sincere describes a wisdom that is both internally pure and externally consistent.
3.18: karpos de dikaiosynēs en eirēnē speiretai — the fruit of righteousness is sown in peace by those who make peace. The metaphor shifts from the immediate crops of earthly wisdom (disorder, chaos) to the harvest of heavenly wisdom (righteousness). Speiretai (is sown, present passive) — the righteousness-harvest is the result of what is sown in peace by peacemakers. The verse rounds off the wisdom-section by connecting peace (mentioned throughout the letter, but notably in 3:17) with the righteousness that James has been concerned with since 1:20. Wisdom produces its fruit in the environment of peace that only genuine wisdom creates.

4.1: hēdonōn hymōn tōn strateuomenōn en tois melesin hymōn — from your pleasures (hēdonōn) that war (strateuomenōn, making war, present participle) among (en) your members. The military metaphor is vivid: the passions are soldiers conducting a campaign in the body — internal warfare producing external conflicts. Hēdonē (pleasure/desire) is used negatively throughout Greek ethical writing for undisciplined desire; it is the root of 'hedonism.' The question pothen (from where?) seeks the origin of the community's conflicts and locates it not in external circumstances but in the inner life of desire.
4.2: epithymeite kai ouk echete... phoneuete kai zēloute kai ou dynasthe epitychein — the sequence desire → lack → murder, covet → cannot obtain → conflict represents an escalating pattern. The use of phoneuete (murder) in a letter to a religious community is unexpected and has generated debate: is it literal, hyperbolic, or metaphorical (hatred as a form of murder, cf. 1 John 3:15)? The Greek machathe kai polemeite (you fight and war) distinguishes personal quarrels (machesthai) from larger organized conflict (polemeite). The diagnosis: desire without prayer produces violence rather than satisfaction.
4.3: katanadapanenēn en tais hēdonais hymōn — that you may spend it on your pleasures (dapanēsēte en tais hēdonais hymōn). The verb dapanaō (spend, use up wastefully) is the same root as the prodigal son's 'squandering' in Luke 15:14 (Greek). Asking wrongly (kakōs) is specifically asking for the purpose of fueling one's desires. James locates the failure not in prayer itself but in its motivation: prayer addressed to God for self-serving pleasures is prayer that treats God as a means to personal gratification rather than as the source and goal of all true blessing.
4.4: moichalides — adulterous people (moichalides, literally 'adulteresses' — the feminine form, used metaphorically for the community's spiritual infidelity). The OT prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) consistently used marriage and adultery as the metaphor for the covenant relationship between YHWH and Israel: idolatry and worldliness are adultery against the divine husband. James picks up this prophetic tradition: friendship with the world (philia tou kosmou) is hostility toward God (echthra tou theou). The world here is not creation but the world-system organized around the desires and values that oppose God.
4.5: pros phthonon epipothei to pneuma ho katōkisen en hēmin — this is the most difficult verse in James. The Greek may be translated: (a) 'The spirit he caused to dwell in us yearns jealously' (God's jealous Spirit longing for our full devotion) or (b) 'The spirit he made to dwell in us craves enviously' (the human spirit tends toward envy). The text is introduced as a scriptural citation (legei hē graphē) but no OT text matches it precisely. Option (a) draws on the OT's pervasive 'jealous God' tradition (Exod 20:5; 34:14; Zech 8:2), where YHWH's jealousy is his fierce covenantal commitment to having his people's full devotion. Most commentators prefer (a): God's Spirit yearns jealously for exclusive devotion from those in whom he dwells.
4.6: meizona de didōsin charin — he gives all the more/greater grace (meizona de didōsin charin). The adversative de (but) marks the contrast with the jealousy of v5: God's jealousy is not punitive hoarding but the passion of one who gives greater grace precisely because he wants full devotion. The citation from Prov 3:34 LXX follows: ho theos hyperēphanois antitassetai, tapeinois de didōsin charin (God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble). The 'proud' (hyperēphanoi — those who display themselves above others) and 'humble' (tapeinoi) recall the rich/poor and the high/low of ch.2. Grace is the counter to the self-assertion that destroys community.
4.7: hypotassō / anthistēmi — submit (hypotassō, arrange yourself under) and resist (anthistēmi, stand against). These are military-administrative verbs: hypotassō is used for troops arranging themselves under a commander; anthistēmi is used for troops standing firm against an advance. The two commands structure the believer's posture in the spiritual warfare described in v1: submission to God (the opposite of the friendship-with-the-world in v4) and resistance to the devil. The promise that the devil will flee (pheuxetai, future tense) follows the pattern of Matt 4:11 (the devil departing after Jesus' resistance in the wilderness) — resistance to temptation is not futile but effective.
4.8: eggisate tō theō kai eggiei hymin — draw near to God and he will draw near to you (eggisate... eggiei). The mutual-approach structure is found in the OT's approach-to-YHWH theology: the covenant relationship is not static but dynamic, and God responds to human turning toward him (Zech 1:3; Mal 3:7: 'return to me, and I will return to you'). The following imperatives — cleanse your hands (katharisate cheiras, cultic hand-washing language) and purify your hearts (hagnisate kardias, the inner cleansing) — apply the outer/inner distinction that the prophets made: Isa 1:15-17 demanded both clean hands and a just heart. Dipsychoi (double-minded, v8) reappears from 1:8: the restoration path for the dipsychos is drawing near to God with a cleansed, undivided heart.
4.9: talaiporēsate kai penthēsate kai klausate — be wretched and mourn and weep (talaiporēsate kai penthēsate kai klausate). The three imperatives describe genuine sorrow over sin — not performative grief but the visceral recognition of the seriousness of the divided-heart condition described in v4-8. Gelōs (laughter) and chara (joy) are to become mourning (penthos) and grief (katēpheia, dejection — the downcast face of genuine remorse). This is not a permanent posture (the promise of v10 is exaltation) but the necessary movement through genuine repentance rather than a cheap turning that bypasses the weight of what has been lost.
4.10: tapeinōthēte enōpion kyriou kai hypsōsei hymas — humble yourselves before the Lord and he will exalt you (hypsōsei, future active — not a possibility but a promise). The passive-voiced humbling (tapeinōthēte, aorist passive imperative) is significant: the person is to allow themselves to be humbled, to place themselves in the lowly position before God. The exaltation is entirely God's act. The same paradox as 1:9-10, the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3), and the Magnificat (Luke 1:52): the lowering of the proud and the lifting of the humble. This promise provides the motive for the painful process of vv8-9: the outcome of drawing near in genuine repentance is not permanent depression but divine exaltation.
4.11: katalaleō / krinōn... ton nomon — to speak evil of (katalaleō, to speak down against) and to judge a brother is to speak evil of and judge the law. The logic: the community lives under the nomos tēs eleutherias (law of liberty) as the governing principle; when you appoint yourself as judge of a brother, you are implicitly positioning yourself above the law (as one who administers it) rather than under it (as one who is governed by it). Ou poiētēs nomou alla kritēs (not a doer of the law but a judge of it) — the contrast between doer (poiētēs) and judge (kritēs) picks up the doer/hearer contrast of 1:22-25.
4.12: heis estin ho nomothetēs kai kritēs — there is one Lawgiver and Judge (heis estin ho nomothetēs kai kritēs). The absolute singularity of the Lawgiver-Judge repeats the Shema's monotheistic logic of 2:19 but applies it to moral governance: just as there is one God, there is one judge, and presuming to judge one's brother is claiming a divine prerogative. Ho dynamenos sōsai kai apolesai (the one able to save and to destroy) — the dual ability echoes Deut 32:39 ('I put to death and I bring to life, I have wounded and I will heal') and Matt 10:28 (the one who can destroy both soul and body in Gehenna). Only this God has the standing to judge.
4.13: age nyn — come now (age nyn) — the same transitional phrase as 5:1. The form is a call to attention, used in Greek rhetoric to introduce a challenging charge. The wealthy merchants are addressed indirectly (not named or addressed directly): their planning speech is quoted ('today or tomorrow we will go to this city...') and then evaluated. The confidence of their commercial planning — the city, the year, the profit — assumes a stability of future they do not possess.
4.14: atmis gar estin hē pros oligon phainomenē, epeita kai aphanizomenē — a mist (atmis) that appears for a little while and then disappears. The word atmis (vapor/mist/steam) is used in LXX Ps 102:3 (NIV 103:3: 'like smoke, my days vanish') and Joel 2:30 (vapor/smoke) for the transience of created existence. The dual participle structure (phainomenē, appearing; aphanizomenē, disappearing — sharing the same stem phain, light/appear) captures the verb visually: the mist appears with brief visibility and then vanishes into invisibility.
4.15: ean ho kyrios thelēsē — if the Lord wills (ean ho kyrios thelēsē). This is the deo volente (God willing) formula, common in both Jewish and early Christian practice. Paul uses it repeatedly (1 Cor 4:19; 16:7; Acts 18:21). The conditional form (ean, if) is not doubt about God's existence but acknowledgment of the contingency of all human planning on the divine will. The contrast with the merchant's bold declarations ('we will go... we will spend... we will make a profit') is sharp: the appropriate grammar for human future plans is subjunctive-conditional, not indicative-declarative.
4.16: kauchāsthe en tais alazoniais hymōn — you boast (kauchāsthe) in your arrogance (alazoniais, plural of alazonia — braggart/self-assertion). Alazonia is used in 1 John 2:16 ('the boastful pride of life') as one of the characteristics of the world-system. The boaster (alazon) in Greek characterization was the person who claimed more than they possessed or more than was true. James identifies commercial planning that ignores God's sovereignty as this kind of hubris: not just imprudent but actively arrogant, claiming a sovereignty over time and outcome that belongs only to God.
4.17: eidoti kalon poiein kai mē poionti — to the one who knows (eidoti) the good to do and does not do it (mē poionti), it is sin. The knowledge-action gap is the Jamesian form of the doer/hearer distinction from 1:22-25. The verse functions as a summary of the chapter: the merchants know the right posture ('if the Lord wills'), the people in vv7-10 know the right response to God (draw near, submit, humble), and yet choosing not to do these known goods is itself a form of sin — not merely ignorance but willful non-action in the face of knowledge.
